So, the question is: How can the mass-loading of business partners be made more efficient?

Data enrichment of large volumes of business partner master data into SAP MDG is critical for organizations managing existing business partner bases or integrating new acquisitions. The CDQ-SAP partnership addresses this challenge with a specialized mass-enrichment feature that allows organizations to efficiently load, validate, and enrich data from trusted sources.

This article outlines the process of mass-loading trusted business partner data into SAP MDG, leveraging CDQ’s data integration to ensure high data quality, reduce manual intervention, and maintain up-to-date records.

What Exactly Is Trusted External Data?

In most countries, businesses must register with local authorities to legally operate, trade, employ workers, or market their services. These registers are the primary source of truth for a company's name, tax obligations, operational locations, and legal structure. Any changes must be officially updated in these registers. The types of registers vary by country: some have both legal and tax registers, some have national registers, while others, particularly federations, maintain separate registers for each state. Despite these differences, nearly all registers share a common challenge: they each have their own data standards, interfaces, and access methods.

Integrating data from these diverse registers can be overwhelming. Even for large organizations, managing integrations with each register is often impractical. This is where CDQ comes in. CDQ integrates both official registers and commercial data providers, mapping their various data fields into a unified standard format. This approach allows businesses to access critical data seamlessly, without the need for a massive, resource-intensive integration project. SAP connects to CDQ's service, embedding this streamlined access directly into their software solutions.

Business Partner Master Data: Mass Enrichment into MDG

When uploading large volumes of trusted data into SAP MDG, handling each record manually isn’t sufficient. SAP and CDQ have developed a mass-enrichment process designed to manage mass loads effectively, whether onboarding new data from acquired entities or updating existing records. CDQ connects to trusted data sources while SAP MDG handles the software side, providing an out-of-the-box solution that minimizes the need for extensive customization.

Key Takeaways

  • Scalability: Mass-enriching addresses the limitations of manual single-record updates, making large-scale data loads efficient and manageable.
  • Data Integrity: The process includes quality checks and data enrichment steps to ensure records are up-to-date and accurate.
  • Ease of Use: The solution is designed for minimal technical support, enabling teams to handle mass loads independently.

More Information

  • Find more information here: Working with SAP Master Data Governance (Cloud-Ready Mode) - Process Steps (SAP Help Portal)

Process Overview

SAP's Cloud Ready Mode includes robust Master Data Management (MDM) processes that enable centralized, high-quality, and governed data across the organization. MDM ensures accurate, consistent data across systems, improves data quality through validation and deduplication, and supports compliance with audit trails and change tracking. MDM also facilitates data synchronization and integration with business processes, enhancing analytics and decision-making.

In this article, we want to look at the “SAP_BP_COA SAP: Consolidate BP Automated” process, which is designed to enhance the information available about business partner master data in SAP MDG. It serves two main purposes:

  1. Onboarding New Business Partners: When a new business partner is added, this process enriches the initial data, ensuring that all relevant information is included from the start. This can include details like contact information, business addresses, and any other important information related to the partner.
  2. Updating Existing Business Partners: For business partners that are already in the system, this process ensures their data is kept up to date. This is especially useful when information changes, such as a company name, location, or other key details, which helps maintain data accuracy and relevance.

An important aspect of this process is that it doesn’t rely on a universal or global identification system to track these partners. Instead, it uses whatever identification numbers already available for each partner (like tax IDs, company registration numbers, or any unique ID the partner has) to identify and enrich their records. This makes it flexible and adaptable to various data sources without requiring new or complex ID systems.

This process handles the complete load of business partner master data for both Initial Loads and Data Updates, making it ideal for supporting Master Data migration projects, ongoing data updates, and the import of business partner master data in mergers and acquisitions (M&A) projects, where integrating data from new entities is critical.

1. Initial Check

The initial check is crucial for setting a solid foundation for the data upload process. This step begins with uploading business partner records using a standardized Excel template. Often, bulk data imports contain diverse data issues such as duplicate records, incomplete entries, internal comments, and outdated information. The initial check process automatically reviews each record, identifying and flagging any missing or inconsistent data fields.

For example, the initial check verifies if all necessary fields are present and identifies where data is missing. It focuses on the quality and format of the input data to ensure consistency and completeness. By ensuring that critical fields like addresses, tax numbers, and company names are properly filled, the initial check helps lay the groundwork for a more effective mass-enrichment process. Only records with all required information proceed, reducing errors and ensuring accurate data quality from the start.

Key Takeaways

  • Data Quality: Prevents common data issues from affecting later steps in the mass-enrichment process.
  • Efficiency: Cleansed input data ensures that resources are not wasted on unnecessary corrections down the line.

More Information

  • Find more information here: Consolidation Process: Initial Check (SAP Help Portal)

2. Reference Data Lookup

This step performs a detailed look-up against external reference databases to identify essential information like tax numbers, registration identifiers, and other legal markers for each business partner. Here, CDQ connects with a variety of trusted data sources based on your chosen configuration. Depending on the sourcing strategy, data may be retrieved from global providers like D&B or Moody’s, free government registers, or specialized data collectors such as the Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) database.

For instance, a German subsidiary of a multinational corporation might be listed in both local German business registers and in a global database with Moody’s. The Reference Data Lookup can cross-reference identifiers across these sources, ensuring that each entry aligns with official records. This validation is vital for detecting duplicate or erroneous entries that may otherwise go unnoticed.

Key Takeaways

  • Data Accuracy: Cross-checks with authoritative sources improve data accuracy and reliability.
  • Flexible Sourcing: Adaptable to your preferred sources, whether international, regional, or industry-specific.

More Information

  • Find more information here: Configure Reference Data Lookup Step (SAP Help Portal)

3. Address Data Validation

During this step, the system verifies and updates address information to ensure alignment with current data from trusted external sources. Often, addresses in a company’s internal records can become outdated as businesses relocate, merge, or undergo other structural changes. The Address Data Validation step matches existing records with the latest official data, allowing users to revalidate and, if necessary, rematch each record.

For example, suppose a company record lists an address that hasn't been updated in years. Through validation, MDG identifies that the company has relocated to a new address or changed its legal structure. The record can then be updated in bulk, ensuring that your address data reflects the most current information. This process can be applied to individual records or to entire data sets, giving users the flexibility to clean up old data systematically.

Key Takeaways

  • Up-to-Date Records: Maintains accurate address data to support contact and regulatory compliance.
  • Improved Data Governance: Reduces data decay by systematically updating outdated entries.

More Information

  • Find more information here: Configure Address Validation for Business Partners  (SAP Help Portal)

4. Matching and Match Group Filtering

In this step, each business partner record in your input file matched with records from CDQ’s trusted data sources. Here’s how it works:

  1. Initial Data Transfer: The process starts by taking key information from each master data record, including the partner's name, address, and any available identifiers (such as tax IDs or registration numbers). This information is sent to CDQ for processing.
  2. Golden Record Creation: CDQ then applies advanced algorithms to create a "golden record" for each entity. This golden record represents the best and most accurate version of the entity, combining information from multiple sources to ensure completeness and reliability.
  3. Returning the Best Match: After creating the golden record, CDQ identifies the best possible match from its trusted sources. It sends back the best candidate record, enriched with the most up-to-date and relevant data, so you have the highest quality information available for each business partner.

This matching process also includes a Match Group Filtering feature. By grouping records based on match quality, it lets users filter results to focus on the most relevant matches and avoid enriching records of inactive entities. This is particularly helpful because many registers flag businesses that are no longer operational, allowing the system to handle such cases efficiently.

For example, during this step, a company with multiple branches might appear as separate entries in various registers, each associated with a slightly different set of data points. MDG can group these records and allow you to select only the active, relevant records for further enrichment. This customization helps avoid overloading your system with unnecessary data and keeps inactive or irrelevant records out of the active database.

Key Takeaways

  • Data Governance: Ensures only active and relevant entities are kept for further processing.
  • Match Customization: Fine-tunes matching criteria to maintain data relevance and quality.

More Information

  • More information here: Configure Matching for Business Partner (SAP Help Portal)

5. Best Record Calculation, Derivation, and Validation

At this stage, CDQ's "golden record" technology aggregates data from multiple sources to generate a comprehensive record that combines the most accurate and relevant information. By chaining identifiers from trusted sources, CDQ creates an enhanced record that pulls in data from various official registers and commercial providers, if available.

For example, if a company is listed in both the official German Commercial Register and D&B with slight variations in registered office addresses or other details, CDQ’s golden record technology will combine these inputs to produce a unified and complete record. This “best of all worlds” approach creates a robust record that minimizes data gaps, allowing organizations to include additional identifiers such as LEIs to strengthen data consistency across international jurisdictions.

Key Takeaways

  • Data Enrichment: Consolidates multiple sources to create a highly detailed, unified record.
  • Real-Time Updates: Pulls data from active sources, ensuring accuracy without relying on outdated static files.

More Information

Find more information here in the SAP Help Portal:

  • Best Record Calculation
  • Derivation
  • Validation

6. Activation

The final activation step prepares enriched records for deployment by allowing users to perform a final quality assessment. SAP MDG summarizes any missing elements or data inconsistencies for each enriched record, providing an overview of potential issues and allowing for a final review. Users can drill down into individual records to examine data points in detail or refine search parameters and enrichment settings if certain records need adjustments.

For instance, suppose the data from a particular register is missing a crucial identifier for a small subset of records. You may choose to manually investigate or rerun specific steps for just those records to ensure complete accuracy. Once satisfied, the enriched data can be activated, replacing outdated records and integrating seamlessly with SAP MDG’s data governance framework.

Key Takeaways

  • Final Quality Assurance: Ensures the activated data meets established quality benchmarks.
  • Flexible Activation: Offers control over which records to activate, allowing for targeted improvements in data governance.

More Information

  • Find more information here: Configure Activation for Business Partner  (SAP Help Portal)

Conclusion

The SAP MDG mass-enrichment feature, in partnership with CDQ’s data integration capabilities, transforms the efficiency of business partner Business partner master data management. By standardizing the approach, SAP and CDQ put the experience of decades of master data projects into a software- and data foundation that can now be used by MDG user organizations. SAP is laying the foundation for an efficiency-gain in large master data projects. With access to a range of reliable international data sources, this solution empowers organizations to implement tailored data sourcing strategies without vendor lock-in. The combination of data quality, flexibility, and ease of use makes this process a valuable asset for both initial data loads and ongoing data updates. Explore our other articles to learn more about leveraging SAP MDG and CDQ for business partner master data Management.

Related Articles:

  • Mass enrichment of business partners with Tax and ID numbers found using the CDQ data provider
  • Hands-on with Free Tiers: CDQ Trusted Business Partner Data in SAP Master Data Governance
  • Trusted Business Partner Data in SAP Master Data Governance, cloud edition

entry.title: Creating a Custom Bank Data Model in SAP MDG (Reuse)
entry.content:
Background

This blog explains how to create a custom data model in SAP Master Data Governance (MDG) with reuse mode. The new data model handles bank master data. The idea of this approach is manually create, update or delete bank master data in the BNKA table within the MDG governance process.

Introduction

Sap MDG provides a robust framework for managing master data. However in some cases, the standard data models may not fully meet your organization’s requirements. This is where
creating a custom data model comes in handy. İn this blog, we will go through the step-by-step process of creating a custom bank data model in sap MDG.

Steps for Creating the Custom Bank Data Model

Step 1: Define the Custom Bank Data Model

  • Navigate to the Data Model Screen:
    To start, you need to create a new data model via transaction MDGIMG under General Settings-> DataModeling->Edit DataModel.


Figure 1 – Edit Data Model

  • Create a Custom Data Model:
    General Settings-> Data Modeling -> Edit Data Model-> Click on New Entries-> Enter data model name and Description and save


Figure 2 – Create Data Model

  • Create a Custom Attributes:
    If required you can entities like Bank with additional attributes, such as bank category or bank type.


Figure 3 – Create Attributes

Step 2: Define the Change Request Type

Nex, you need to define the change request type that will be used for creating and managing the bank data in this new model.

  • Define Change Request Type:
    In MDGIMG, navigate to General Settings->Process Modeling->Change Requests-> Create Change Request Type


Figure 4 – Create Change Request Type

Create a new change request type specifically for your custom model (e.g., ZBNK01).
Assign the custom data model you created to this change request type, ensure that the correct workflow and approval process are configured for this change request type.

Step 3: Configure the Fiori Catalog and Group

To make your custom model accessible in Fiori configure a catalog and group.

  • Create Fiori Catalog and Group:
    In the Fiori Launchpad Designer, create a catalog (e.g., zbnkidct ) and create a group (e.g., zbnkidgrp).


Figure 5 – Create Fiori Catalog

Step 4: Create a Custom UI in SAP Fiori

Navigate to General Settings->UI Modeling->Manage UI Configurations.

A custom UI in sap fiori will enhance the user experience by providing a structured interface for interacting with the data model.


Figure 6 – Create Custom UI

Testing

Once the configuration is complete, perform testing to ensure the data model and UI are functioning as expected.


Figure 7 – Search Result


Figure 8 – Create new bank entry


Figure 9 – BNKA table result

Conclusion

With fiori integration, workflow management field-level, you can ensure a streamlined, user-friendly experience.

Testing ensures all configurations are working as expected.enhancing data quaity and operational efficiency. If you have questions or tips to share, feel free to leave a comment below!

Thank you for reading,
Best Regards.

Ahmet

entry.title: Release Navigator for SAP BTP - What' s New in Q4 2024
entry.content:

Check it out.

Check it out.

3. SAP Translation Hub

This page contains links to release schedule information, SAP Roadmap Explorer, What’s New information in the SAP Help Portal, links to the SAP Community, and helpful documentation.

Check it out.

Fig 3. The new page for SAP Translation Hub.

 

Currently, the release navigator for SAP BTP covers content for 30 products and services on dedicated pages. For all products and services not featured with their own page you can find helpful resources on the new General page.

Expect more new pages dedicated to specific products and services in future updates and don't hesitate to let us know via our feedback survey, which products or services you would like to see added next.

Regarding Release Navigator updates, you can refer to this update plan:

*Disclaimer:  The schedule is subject to change without prior notice including, when necessary, delays or incorporating changes in subsequent update windows and not a commitment, promise, or legal obligation to deliver any material, code, or functionality.

Looking ahead, 2025 updates will mainly focus on quarterly maintenance of release resources and a steady extension of product coverage. Currently, there are no plans for further mayor UI updates in the near future.

 

The Readiness@Scale team at SAP Enterprise Support is dedicated to ensuring release readiness across the SAP Enterprise Support portfolio. Our mission is to empower SAP customers to be prepared and ready to adopt and utilize innovations.

If you have any feedback or specific requests for content in the release navigator, please don't hesitate to reach out to us.

 

And don't forget to check out our team's blogs tagged with Readiness@Scale.

Happy navigating!

Readiness@Scale 
SAP Enterprise Support

entry.title: Master Data Management: The Foundation for Modern Enterprise Data Architecture
entry.content:
Within an enterprise, critical data such as customer information, product details, asset records, and location data is used across various business units and operational systems. Managing this "master data" to ensure its accuracy, timeliness, and consistency is the core function of Master Data Management (MDM). This process, complemented by robust data governance practices, ensures that the responsibility for data quality is appropriately managed and verified. Over the past two decades, the software industry has provided systems to support MDM, but what functionalities are essential for a modern MDM system today?

Core Functions of a Modern MDM System

Data Integration and Cleansing

A modern MDM system should access data from operational systems and bring it together in a master data hub. This involves loading and synchronizing data, cleansing it to correct errors, and enriching records—for instance, filling missing postal codes, tax IDs or adding demographic information – leveraging data quality services, like SAP Data Quality Management, microservices for location data, or third party data providers, like CDQ (Corporate Data Quality).

Data Matching and Merging

With master data distributed across numerous systems, identifying duplicate records is crucial. The system should be capable of merging candidate records into a single, trusted master record (sometimes called the “best record” or “golden record”) through predefined rules based on criteria like how recently they were updated or their completeness and, sometimes, human intervention.

Data Governance and Workflow

A modern MDM system should support data governance processes, including access controls, data ownership, and audit trails. Automated, customizable workflow features for data stewards to manage data issues and approvals for changes are also essential. This allows various teams to own unique master data attributes and enforce validated values for specific data points through collaborative workflow routing and notification.

Hierarchy and Classification Management

Managing hierarchies of classification (e.g., legal business hierarchies, retail shop locations, customer classifications) is a key feature. The system should handle complex, unbalanced hierarchies efficiently, including the ability to retrieve legal hierarchies from external data providers like a Dun & Bradstreet so that the MDM system can load that data, solve any conflicts, and replicate the hierarchy information to consuming applications.

Analytics and Reporting

The system should provide intuitive analytics and reporting capabilities, highlighting data quality metrics and trends over time.  The MDM solution should allow data experts to define, validate, and monitor established business rules to confirm master data readiness and quality.  There should also be the ability analyze the performance of master data management processes (i.e. number of open master data change requests or the average time to close a change request).

Scalability and Deployment Options

A modern MDM system should be scalable and support both on-premise and cloud-based deployment options, adapting as source data migrates between different platforms.

Security and Usability

The system should have role-based security, ensuring only authorized users can perform specific tasks. An intuitive, customizable user interface and industry or data domain-specific templates can speed up deployment.

Supporting Current Initiatives

A robust MDM system supports digitalization, cloud migration, and business process automation. It also provides high-quality data for training AI models, making the AI more effective. As businesses increasingly adopt generative AI, having a reliable MDM system ensures that the AI models are trained on accurate, trustworthy data – and the most important data is your business-critical master data.

MDM and Data Fabric

The data fabric approach aims to provide business users direct access to corporate data assets without traditional data warehouses. However, the semantic layer of a data fabric must navigate numerous underlying operational systems to determine the most up-to-date and trustworthy data. An MDM system is instrumental here, as it carries out the critical work of cleaning, merging, and enriching data to create "golden records." By using master data from the MDM hub, the data fabric's semantic layer can navigate enterprise data assets more effectively, simplifying queries and providing accurate, consistent data for business users.

MDM as a Foundation for Generative AI

Generative AI, based on large language models (LLMs), has the potential to revolutionize various industries. However, LLMs frequently produce unreliable content due to poor-quality training data from publicly available sources. Corporations are now training LLMs on corporate-specific data for better accuracy.

MDM systems provide the high-quality, timely, and accurate data needed to train these LLMs effectively. Companies with well-managed MDM hubs have a competitive advantage, as their AI models perform significantly better with reliable data foundations.

Conclusion

Master data management remains a cornerstone of modern enterprise data architecture. Whether implementing a data fabric or training generative AI, having a well-managed master data hub is essential. The ability to access, clean, and enrich data ensures that enterprises can leverage advanced technologies effectively, driving innovation and maintaining a competitive edge. Investing in MDM is not just a safeguard for data quality; it is a strategic move that underpins numerous modern data initiatives.

Read the full paper on the capabilities and benefits of a modern master data management system here and listen to our podcast.

Learn more about SAP’s solution for master data management, SAP Master Data Governance, at www.sap.com/mdg.

entry.title: MDG DQM Validation Rules – Access additional data model tables without ABAP coding
entry.content:
Background

If you want to implement medium or complex validations it’s often necessary to involve data and attribute values from tables that are not available by direct access in the rule. In the past (BAdI/coding approach) this was also called "cross entity" and it caused enhanced coding and data model knowledge. In MDG DQM this is now very easy because the framework gives you the ability to access the data by using predefined procedure calls.

This blog explains how you can access additional data model tables using these procedure calls so that this implementation can be used in all MDG processes – even to access current change request data.

Introduction

When the system calls the implementation of a validation rule, the data of the base table, the parent table of the base table, and the root table is automatically supplied.

An example for the business partner data model: If the base table is Phone (ADR2), the data from the Address (ADRC) and the General Data (BUT000) tables is automatically supplied.

An example for the product data model: If the base table is Storage Location (MARD), the data from the Plant Data (MARC) and the Basic Data (MARA) tables is automatically supplied.

But how to access data that resides in a different table of the data model?

The answer in a nutshell: Use the delivered procedure calls in BRFplus in the applications ZMDQ_147 (for business partners) and ZMDQ_194 (for products).

Steps for creating a validation rule using delivered procedure call

Let’s take an example: Create a validation rule that checks that for a business partner of category Organization either the tax number category DE0 or DE1 is assigned. This rule shall be executed in a change request.

Step 1: Create validation rule with base table General Data (BUT000)

Use the Validation Rules for Business Partners app to create a rule with some business details and base table General Data (BUT000).

Note that the base table is not Tax Numbers (DFKKBPTAXNUM) for the following reason: During execution the system loops over all entries of the rule base table – but if there is no entry (in this example: if there is no tax number at all), no error is raised.

Step 2: Add rule usage and prepare BRFplus artefacts

Add rule usage Check in Change Requests and choose Prepare.

Step 3: Implement rule scope Partner Category is equal to Organization

Navigate to the BRFplus Workbench using the link for the rule scope expression in the Implementation section. Select the field Partner Category from the context and set the value to ‘2’ (Organization).

Step 4: Implement rule condition Assigned Tax Number Category is equal to DE0 or DE1

In the following, we explain how to access data from the process table DFKKBPTAXNUM_PRC creating a table operation that uses the delivered procedure call DFKKBPTAXNUM lookup.

  • Navigate to the BRFplus Workbench using the link for the rule condition expression in the Implementation section.
  • Create a table operation:

 

 Choose Create and Navigate to Object and confirm the upcoming popup windows. 

 

Choose the operation Has at least.

To access data from table DFKKBPTAXNUM select the procedure call DFKKBPTAXNUM lookup.

 

With the next step, you need to define the selection conditions for the tax type category.

Do not forget to save your expression.

Note: Process ID, Step Number, Source System and Source ID are automatically filled during execution.

Step 5: Approve rule and enable usage

Go back to the Validation Rules app, approve the rule and enable the desired usages.

Step 6: Test rule in a change request

Using this procedure call ensures that the current data is checked for the different usages. This includes the change request as well and saves you from having to call up the change request API.

Using the Check action in a change request shows the predefined message if the data violates the rule:

Summary

These procedure calls are available for the other data model tables as well. If you cannot find them in your BRFplus application you might need to update the BRFplus infrastructure using report MDQ_UPD_INFRASTRUCTURE_VAL. If the report is not yet available in your system refer to note 3279689.

Using these procedure calls will simplify the implementation of your validation rules. As mentioned before – they are available for product master data as well.

Cheers
